section.title: Amnon and Tamar
section.sectionRationale: The consequences Nathan announced begin to fall inside David's own house. His son Amnon, aided by a shrewd cousin, lures and rapes his half-sister Tamar, then throws her out in cold hatred. David is furious but does nothing, and Tamar is left desolate. Two years later Absalom, her brother, takes revenge by murdering Amnon at a feast and flees into exile. The movement is a harrowing account of lust, violence, injustice, and vengeance, the sword rising within David's family exactly as the LORD had warned.
section.thesis: The sword promised to David's house begins its work through his own children: Amnon's lust becomes rape and then hatred, David's failure to act leaves evil unpunished and a victim desolate, and Absalom's smoldering revenge becomes murder, showing how sin unaddressed festers into deeper ruin across a family.
section.frameNote: Three beats: Amnon's scheme and the rape of Tamar, verses 1 to 19; the desolation of Tamar and David's inaction, verses 20 to 22; and Absalom's revenge on Amnon and flight to Geshur, verses 23 to 39.

units.label: Amnon's crime against Tamar
units.note: such a thing should never be done in Israel

verses.text: After some time, David’s son Amnon fell in love with Tamar, the beautiful sister of David’s son Absalom.
Amnon was sick with frustration over his sister Tamar, for she was a virgin, and it seemed implausible for him to do anything to her.
Now Amnon had a friend named Jonadab, the son of David’s brother Shimeah. Jonadab was a very shrewd man,
and he asked Amnon, “Why are you, the son of the king, so depressed morning after morning? Won’t you tell me?” Amnon replied, “I am in love with Tamar, my brother Absalom’s sister.”

Jonadab told him, “Lie down on your bed and pretend you are ill. When your father comes to see you, say to him, ‘Please let my sister Tamar come and give me something to eat. Let her prepare it in my sight so I may watch her and eat it from her hand.’”
So Amnon lay down and feigned illness. When the king came to see him, Amnon said, “Please let my sister Tamar come and make a couple of cakes in my sight, so that I may eat from her hand.”
Then David sent word to Tamar at the palace: “Please go to the house of Amnon your brother and prepare a meal for him.”
So Tamar went to the house of her brother Amnon, who was lying down. She took some dough, kneaded it, made cakes in his sight, and baked them.
Then she brought the pan and set it down before him, but he refused to eat. “Send everyone away!” said Amnon. And everyone went out.
Then Amnon said to Tamar, “Bring the food into the bedroom, so that I may eat it from your hand.” Tamar took the cakes she had made and went to her brother Amnon’s bedroom.
And when she had brought them to him to eat, he took hold of her and said, “Come lie with me, my sister!”
“No, my brother!” she cried. “Do not violate me, for such a thing should never be done in Israel. Do not do this {{nebalah|disgraceful thing}}!
Where could I ever take my shame? And you would be like one of the fools in Israel! Please speak to the king, for he will not withhold me from you.”
But Amnon refused to listen to her, and being stronger, he violated her and lay with her.
Then Amnon {{sane|hated}} Tamar with such intensity that his hatred was greater than the love he previously had. “Get up!” he said to her. “Be gone!”
“No,” she replied, “sending me away is worse than this great wrong you have already done to me!” But he refused to listen to her.
Instead, he called to his attendant and said, “Throw this woman out and bolt the door behind her!”
So Amnon’s attendant threw her out and bolted the door behind her. Now Tamar was wearing a robe of many colors, because this is what the king’s virgin daughters wore.
And Tamar put ashes on her head and tore her robe. And putting her hand on her head, she went away crying aloud.

units.label: Tamar desolate, David silent
units.note: the king was furious but did nothing

verses.text: Her brother Absalom said to her, “Has your brother Amnon been with you? Be quiet for now, my sister. He is your brother. Do not take this thing to heart.” So Tamar lived as a desolate woman in the house of her brother Absalom.
When King David heard all this, he was furious.
And Absalom never said a word to Amnon, either good or bad, because he hated Amnon for violating his sister Tamar.

units.label: Absalom's revenge
units.note: the sword rises in David's house

verses.text: Two years later, when Absalom’s sheepshearers were at Baal-hazor near Ephraim, he invited all the sons of the king.
And he went to the king and said, “Your servant has just hired shearers. Will the king and his servants please come with me?”
“No, my son,” the king replied, “we should not all go, or we would be a burden to you.” Although Absalom urged him, he was not willing to go, but gave him his blessing.
“If not,” said Absalom, “please let my brother Amnon go with us.” “Why should he go with you?” the king asked.
But Absalom urged him, so the king sent Amnon and the rest of his sons.

Now Absalom had ordered his young men, “Watch Amnon until his heart is merry with wine, and when I order you to strike Amnon down, you are to kill him. Do not be afraid. Have I not commanded you? Be courageous and valiant!”
So Absalom’s young men did to Amnon just as Absalom had ordered. Then all the other sons of the king got up, and each one fled on his mule.
While they were on the way, a report reached David: “Absalom has struck down all the sons of the king; not one of them is left!”
Then the king stood up, tore his clothes, and lay down on the ground. And all his servants stood by with their clothes torn.

But Jonadab, the son of David’s brother Shimeah, spoke up: “My lord must not think they have killed all the sons of the king, for only Amnon is dead. In fact, Absalom has planned this since the day Amnon violated his sister Tamar.
So now, my lord the king, do not take to heart the report that all the sons of the king are dead. Only Amnon is dead.”

Meanwhile, Absalom had fled. When the young man standing watch looked up, he saw many people coming down the road west of him, along the side of the hill. And the watchman went and reported to the king, “I see men coming from the direction of Horonaim, along the side of the hill.”
So Jonadab said to the king, “Look, the sons of the king have arrived! It is just as your servant said.”
And as he finished speaking, the sons of the king came in, wailing loudly. Then the king and all his servants also wept very bitterly.
Now Absalom fled and went to Talmai son of Ammihud, the king of Geshur. But David mourned for his son every day.
After Absalom had fled and gone to Geshur, he stayed there three years.
And King David longed to go to Absalom, for he had been consoled over Amnon’s death.

nebalah.gloss: A disgraceful outrage, senseless folly, a wicked act that violates the moral order of God's people. Tamar names Amnon's intended act a nebalah, 'such a thing should never be done in Israel,' the same grave word used for the worst crimes in the law. It marks the deed not as a private wrong but as an assault on the covenant community's most sacred bonds.

sane.gloss: To hate, to reject with hostility. What Amnon called love was only lust, and once his desire is spent he sane, hated Tamar, with a hatred greater than his supposed love. The word exposes the difference between selfish craving and true love: craving discards its object, while love seeks the good of the other. Amnon's hatred adds cruelty to violence, casting Tamar out to compound her ruin.

basic.context: Nathan had told David that because of his sin the sword would never leave his house and that evil would rise against him from his own family. Chapter 13 shows the first terrible installment. David's oldest son Amnon becomes obsessed with his beautiful half-sister Tamar. His cunning cousin Jonadab devises a scheme: Amnon feigns illness and asks that Tamar come and cook for him. David, unwittingly, sends her. Alone with her, Amnon seizes Tamar. She pleads with him not to commit such a disgraceful outrage, offering any other path, but he overpowers and rapes her. Then, in a chilling turn, his lust curdles into hatred, and he has her thrown out and the door bolted. Tamar tears the royal robe of the virgin princesses, puts ashes on her head, and is left a desolate woman in her brother Absalom's house. David hears and is furious but, tellingly, does nothing to punish Amnon or restore Tamar. Absalom says nothing to Amnon but nurses his hatred for two years, then lures Amnon to a sheepshearing feast and has him murdered, before fleeing into exile at Geshur. The chapter raises hard questions about lust masquerading as love, the failure of a father to do justice, the desolation of a victim, and the ruin that spreads when sin is neither confronted nor healed.
basic.hermeneutics: This is unflinching historical narrative, description of grievous evil, never its endorsement. Tamar is portrayed with dignity and given a voice; the text clearly condemns Amnon's crime as a disgraceful outrage and exposes his hatred and cruelty. David's inaction is reported as a failure, an ancient manuscript tradition even noting he would not punish Amnon because he loved him as his firstborn. The chapter must not be read as blaming Tamar; the guilt is entirely Amnon's, and later Absalom's for his murder. The passage teaches by showing the bitter fruit of unrestrained lust, injustice, and vengeance within a family under God's announced discipline.
basic.originalLanguages: Tamar names the intended crime with the gravest word available, nebalah, a disgraceful outrage, 'such a thing should never be done in Israel,' the term the law reserves for acts that tear the fabric of God's people. And the narrator exposes the lie beneath Amnon's so-called love with a single verb: once his desire is satisfied he sane, hated Tamar, more than he had ever 'loved' her. Together the words tell the truth of the scene, a wicked outrage driven not by love but by lust that, spent, turns to hatred and cruelty.
basic.typology: This chapter offers no positive type but a dark negative one: the sons of David here are the opposite of the true Son of David. Amnon takes and defiles by force, then hates; the true Son of David gives Himself in love and never casts out the one who comes to Him. Tamar, the innocent sufferer left desolate and unavenged by an earthly king's inaction, cries out for a King who will do justice for the violated and the crushed, a longing answered only in Christ.
basic.christ: The horror of this chapter drives us to long for the true King. Where Amnon's craving used and discarded, Christ's love lays down His life for His bride and will never cast out the one who comes to Him. Where David failed to do justice for his violated daughter, Christ is the righteous Judge who sees every violation, defends the crushed, and will right every wrong. And where sin's sword ran through David's house because sin was covered rather than confronted, Christ deals with sin fully at the cross, breaking its power to spread and destroy.
basic.god: God is not named as acting in this chapter, and His silence is part of its horror, yet the whole scene unfolds under the discipline He had announced through Nathan, so that even this evil is within, not outside, His sovereign warning to David. God is the God who hears the cry of the desolate like Tamar, who hates the outrage that men commit, and who will not leave injustice unanswered forever. The chapter's darkness makes us long for the God who judges rightly and heals the broken.
basic.crossReferences: Second Samuel 12 gives Nathan's warning that the sword would not leave David's house and evil rise from within it, now being fulfilled. Genesis 34 uses the same word 'disgraceful thing' for the violation of Dinah. Leviticus 18 forbids the incest Amnon commits. And Christ's promise never to cast out the one who comes to Him, John 6, stands in stark contrast to Amnon casting out Tamar.
basic.oneStory: The sword the LORD warned of begins to fall within David's own family, as lust becomes rape, injustice leaves a victim desolate, and hatred becomes murder. This harrowing chapter exposes the ruin that sin spreads when it is neither confronted nor healed, and it makes us ache for the true Son of David, who does not defile but redeems, who does not cast out but welcomes, and who will do perfect justice for every Tamar the world has crushed.

context.literary: The chapter deliberately mirrors chapter 11: like his father, Amnon 'takes' a woman by the abuse of power, and the family reaps what David sowed. It is structured around Tamar's tragedy and Absalom's revenge, with the shrewd Jonadab framing both the crime and the aftermath. The torn royal robe and ashes mark Tamar's grief, and the two-year silence of Absalom builds to the explosion of murder at the feast, echoing how deferred, unaddressed evil erupts.
context.historical: Marriages between half-siblings were forbidden in the law, though Tamar's plea that David 'will not withhold me from you' may reflect either desperation or a hope for some royal exception. A robe of many colors, like Joseph's, marked the virgin daughters of the king. Sheepshearing was a festive occasion with feasting and wine, a fitting cover for Absalom's ambush. Fleeing to Geshur, the kingdom of Absalom's maternal grandfather, gave him refuge beyond David's reach.
context.canonical: This chapter begins the fulfillment of the judgment of chapter 12 and launches the Absalom narrative that dominates chapters 13 to 19. It stands in Scripture as a sober, honest account of sexual violence and family breakdown, refusing to sanitize the sins of the great. Tamar's dignity and voice, and the clear condemnation of the outrage against her, resonate with the Bible's wider concern for justice for the violated and vulnerable.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show the sword entering David's own house through his children, as Nathan foretold, exposing the ruin that lust, injustice, and vengeance bring, and portraying with honesty both the outrage committed against Tamar and the failures of the men who should have protected and vindicated her.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter is entirely descriptive of sin and its consequences, never prescriptive; it condemns Amnon's crime and exposes David's inaction and Absalom's vengeance rather than commending any of them. What endures is the truth that lust is not love, that covered and unpunished evil festers and spreads, that victims like Tamar must be seen and defended, and that human justice fails where God's justice is needed.

debates.question: Why does David, though furious, do nothing to punish Amnon or restore Tamar?

views.label: A failure of justice rooted in David's compromised authority
views.case: David is 'furious' but takes no action, and an ancient reading preserved in the Dead Sea Scrolls and Septuagint adds that he would not punish Amnon because he loved him as his firstborn; David's own sin with Bathsheba had undercut his moral authority to judge his son's sexual crime, so his paralysis is best read as a real failure that leaves Tamar desolate and emboldens Absalom to take justice into his own hands.

views.label: Weak parental partiality with tragic results
views.case: Others emphasize David's pattern of indulgent, passive parenting seen across these chapters, failing to restrain or discipline his sons; whether from favoritism, guilt, or weakness, his inaction is a dereliction of the king's duty to do justice, and the narrative presents it as a cause of the deepening disaster, not as wise restraint.

debates.bearing: Either way the text presents David's inaction as a grievous failure, not a virtue; a victim is left without justice and a murderer is left unchecked, illustrating how a leader's compromised authority and passivity allow evil to spread, and heightening the longing for the righteous King who will judge justly and defend the crushed.

study.originalLanguages: Tamar reaches for the strongest moral word in her vocabulary to name what Amnon intends: nebalah, a disgraceful outrage, insisting 'such a thing should never be done in Israel,' verse 12. This is the term the law uses for crimes that rip the fabric of the covenant community, marking the deed not as a private matter but as an assault on Israel's most sacred bonds. Then the narrator strips away the pretense of Amnon's 'love' with one brutal verb: having satisfied his lust, he sane, hated Tamar, and his hatred was greater than his so-called love, verse 15. The vocabulary tells the whole truth of the scene. What paraded as love was only craving; craving, once fed, discards and despises its object, so Amnon adds cruelty to violence by casting Tamar out. The words expose the chasm between lust, which uses and hates, and love, which gives and seeks the good of the other, and they leave no doubt where the guilt lies.

typology.type: The son of David who takes and casts out by force
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true Son of David, who gives Himself in love and never casts out the one who comes to Him, John 6:37, the exact opposite of Amnon.

typology.type: The innocent sufferer left desolate and unavenged
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who takes up the cause of the crushed and will do perfect justice for every victim of violence, Luke 18:7-8.

typology.type: The sword rising within the house because sin was covered
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who deals with sin fully at the cross rather than letting it fester and spread, Colossians 2:14-15.

study.christ: The horror of this chapter drives us to long for the true King, and every dark contrast points to Him. Where Amnon's craving used Tamar and then discarded her in hatred, Christ's love lays down His life for His bride and declares that the one who comes to Him He will never cast out, John 6:37. Where David, though furious, failed to do justice for his violated daughter, Christ is the righteous Judge who sees every violation, hears the cry of the crushed, and will vindicate them, Luke 18:7-8. And where the sword ran through David's house because sin was covered rather than confronted, Christ confronts and bears sin fully at the cross, disarming its power to spread and destroy, Colossians 2:14-15. The chapter's unrelieved darkness is itself a summons to the Redeemer who does what these sons of David could not.
study.god: God is not named as acting in this chapter, and His apparent silence is part of its horror, yet the whole scene unfolds within the discipline He had announced through Nathan, so that even this evil lies inside, not outside, His sovereign warning to David. God is the one who hears the cry of the desolate, like Tamar left crying aloud with ashes on her head, who hates the outrage that men commit against the vulnerable, and who will not leave injustice unanswered forever. The very absence of an immediate divine rescue deepens the reader's longing for the God who judges rightly, defends the violated, and heals the broken, a longing that Scripture answers fully only in Christ.

crossReferences.note: Nathan's warning that the sword would never leave David's house and that evil would rise from within it, now beginning to be fulfilled.
The law forbidding sexual relations with a sister or half-sister, which Amnon violates.
The same word 'disgraceful thing,' nebalah, used of the violation of Dinah, marking such acts as outrages against Israel.
Christ's promise that the one who comes to Him He will never cast out, in stark contrast to Amnon casting Tamar out.

study.oneStory: The sword the LORD warned of begins to fall within David's own family, as Amnon's lust becomes rape, David's inaction leaves a violated daughter desolate and unvindicated, and Absalom's nursed hatred becomes murder. This harrowing chapter lays bare the ruin that sin spreads when it is neither confronted nor healed, and it makes the reader ache for the true Son of David, who does not defile but redeems, who does not cast out but welcomes, and who will do perfect justice for every Tamar the world has crushed. The darkness here is the shadow that makes the coming light of Christ shine all the brighter.

quiz.q: How does Amnon commit his crime against Tamar?

options.text: He feigns illness on Jonadab's advice, gets Tamar sent to cook for him, then overpowers and rapes her despite her pleas.
options.note: Verses 5-14 record the scheme and the assault against Tamar's clear protest.

options.text: He openly asks David for Tamar's hand and is refused, then takes her by force.
options.note: Amnon does not seek marriage openly; he schemes with Jonadab to get Tamar alone and assaults her.

options.text: He lures Tamar to his house under a false pretext and violates her.
options.note: True, but this option omits the role of Jonadab's scheme and David's unwitting part in sending Tamar.

options.text: He is seduced by Tamar and gives in to temptation.
options.note: The text places all guilt on Amnon; Tamar pleads against the crime and is the victim, not a seducer.

quiz.q: What does Amnon's treatment of Tamar after the assault reveal?

options.text: That his 'love' was only lust, for once his desire was spent he hated her and cruelly cast her out.
options.note: Verse 15 exposes his hatred, greater than his supposed love, and his cruelty in expelling her.

options.text: That he genuinely loved her but was ashamed of what he had done.
options.note: The text says he hated her intensely, the opposite of love or godly shame.

options.text: That his desire turned to hatred once it was satisfied.
options.note: True, but this option omits the added cruelty of throwing Tamar out and bolting the door against her.

options.text: That he immediately regretted his sin and sought to make it right.
options.note: He shows no repentance; he compounds the wrong by casting Tamar out to deepen her ruin.

quiz.q: How does David respond when he hears what Amnon did?

options.text: He is furious but does nothing to punish Amnon or restore Tamar, a failure of justice.
options.note: Verse 21 shows David's anger without action, leaving the crime unaddressed.

options.text: He immediately puts Amnon on trial and has him punished.
options.note: The opposite; David takes no action against Amnon at all.

options.text: He is angry but fails to act, leaving the wrong unaddressed.
options.note: True, but this option omits that a victim was left desolate and a murderer, Absalom, was thereby emboldened.

options.text: He comforts Tamar personally and restores her honor.
options.note: David does not restore Tamar; she is left desolate in Absalom's house while David does nothing.

quiz.q: How does Absalom respond to the outrage against his sister?

options.text: He says nothing to Amnon for two years, then lures him to a feast, has him murdered, and flees into exile.
options.note: Verses 22-38 record Absalom's patient, cold revenge and flight to Geshur.

options.text: He forgives Amnon and seeks reconciliation within the family.
options.note: The opposite; Absalom nurses hatred and eventually kills Amnon.

options.text: He takes revenge by having Amnon killed at a feast.
options.note: True, but this option omits the chilling two-year silence in which his hatred was concealed and premeditated.

options.text: He appeals to David to bring Amnon to justice through the courts.
options.note: Absalom does not appeal to David; he takes vengeance into his own hands.

quiz.q: How does this dark chapter point us toward Christ?

options.text: By contrast: where these sons of David defile, cast out, and fail to do justice, the true Son of David loves, welcomes, and judges rightly for the crushed.
options.note: The chapter's evils make us long for Christ, John 6:37 and Luke 18:7-8.

options.text: By showing that God's people should take revenge into their own hands as Absalom did.
options.note: The chapter condemns the whole chain of sin, including Absalom's murder; it does not commend vengeance.

options.text: By making us long for a King who will do justice for the violated and never cast them out.
options.note: True, but this option omits the sharp contrast with Amnon's defiling and casting out that so clearly points to Christ.

options.text: By proving that God had abandoned David's house entirely.
options.note: God had not abandoned the covenant; the evil unfolds within His announced discipline, and grace still runs toward Christ.

questions: Amnon called his craving love, but it used Tamar and then despised her. How can you tell the difference between selfish desire and love that seeks another's good?
David was furious but did nothing, leaving Tamar without justice. Where might God be calling you to act, and not merely to feel strongly, on behalf of someone who has been wronged?
Tamar was left desolate, her cry unheard by those who should have defended her. How does it comfort you that God sees and hears the crushed even when people fail them?
Absalom hid his hatred for two years until it erupted in murder. Is there resentment you are nursing in silence, and what would it mean to bring it into the light before it destroys?
This chapter drives us to long for the true Son of David who never casts out those who come to Him. How does Christ's welcome of the wounded and violated shape the way you treat them?

translationNotes.note: Tamar calls the intended act a nebalah, a disgraceful outrage or wicked folly, the grave term the law uses for crimes that violate the covenant community, as in Genesis 34:7.

The BSB footnote notes the 'robe of many colors' can be rendered a long-sleeved robe, the garment worn by the king's virgin daughters, recalling Joseph's robe in Genesis 37:3; Tamar's tearing of it signals her lost status and grief.

The BSB notes that the Dead Sea Scrolls and Septuagint add, after 'he was furious,' the words 'But he would not punish his son Amnon, because he loved him, since he was his firstborn,' explaining David's failure to act; the BSB main text carries the shorter Hebrew reading.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Samuel 13:1-39. No em dashes or en dashes appear in the source text for this chapter; none introduced in this file. The verse 21 addition found in DSS and LXX, absent from the BSB main text, is carried in the translationNotes. Sexual violence is handled as honest description that condemns the crime and honors the victim, not prescription.
